## What It Does Well ✅

- **Real strategy** -- reads your actual active backtester strategy from agent-network, not a generic one

## What It Doesn't Simulate ❌

- **No slippage** -- fills at exact price, real trades slip especially on SPX500
- **No spread** -- CFD/broker spread costs not modeled
- **No partial fills** -- assumes full fill every time
- **EOD data only** -- yfinance gives daily candles, not intraday ticks, so signals fire at close price

For **broker accuracy** -- what would my actual P&L be with a real account? -- it will be optimistic (no spread/slippage costs).

**It's a good fit for what you're doing.** You're validating a strategy that already has backtester metrics (Sharpe 0.943, 39.1% win rate). Virtual-trader gives you a live forward-test to see if those numbers hold up in real market conditions.

## Answering Your Question Properly

### On Market-Scan Frequency

**The current setup runs hourly and that's correct for now**, but here's the full reasoning:

Your strategy (EMA38, Bollinger Band, ATR) is built on **daily candles**. A "signal" only has meaning once a full day's candle has closed. Running market-scan every 15 minutes won't produce new signals -- it'll just re-read the same incomplete candle and output the same result.

**What hourly scans are actually doing right now:**
- Checking crypto prices (BTC/ETH/BNB) for the executive dashboard
- Detecting major intraday moves for alerts
- That's it -- they don't feed into virtual-trader

**What would justify more frequent scans:**
- Intraday strategies (1hr or 4hr candles) -- not your current approach
- Live broker execution where you want to enter/exit mid-day near a price level
- Stop-loss monitoring intraday so a position doesn't blow past its stop before close

**The gap I flagged:** market-scan currently scans crypto only. It doesn't pull SPX500 or Gold at all. So even the hourly runs aren't watching your actual instruments. That's worth fixing -- but frequency isn't the issue, the watchlist is.

Good question. Here's how it actually works:

---

## There's Only One Machine: The PC

**"Mobile" doesn't mean a separate git repo.** When you make changes from mobile, you're remotely controlling Claude Code which runs **on the PC**. All file edits and commits happen on Tyler's PC locally.

So the git flow is:
```
Your mobile phone
      ↓ (you type instructions)
  Claude Code (running on PC)
      ↓ (makes file edits + commits)
  PC local git repo
      ↓ (git push)
  GitHub (Z_Claw remote)
```

---

## Why the Conflicts Happened

The conflict today was caused by **two separate Claude Code sessions** making commits to the same files without a sync in between:

1. **Mobile session** committed changes to `trading-report/SKILL.md` → pushed to GitHub
2. **Another session** also changed `trading-report/SKILL.md` locally on PC → **didn't push yet**

Now PC local and GitHub had **different versions of the same file** → merge conflict.

---

## Going Forward

Once the Git Sync button is built, you tap it after any coding session and it auto-resolves this. Until then, the rule is: **push to GitHub after every session** and conflicts won't stack up like this.

## Agent Network -- Full Architecture

### How It Runs (The Cycle)
Each cycle runs 4 agents in sequence, orchestrated by `cycle/orchestrator.py`:

```
Trading Coach → Strategy Builder → Backtester → Trader
```

---

### Agent 1: **Trading Coach** (`agents/trading_coach.py`)
**Role:** Fund manager / performance analyst

- **Runs first** each cycle using data from the previous cycle
- Computes **weekly + monthly performance reviews** per strategy
- Assigns **health tiers**: Good / Marginal / Poor
- Sets **risk multipliers** per strategy (Good: normal, Marginal: 0.75x, Poor: 0.5x)
- **Retires strategies** after 3 consecutive losing weeks
- Manages **compounding mode** progression (no-compound → monthly → per-trade) based on months at Good tier
- Uses Claude LLM to generate a **narrative hypothesis** for the next strategy batch
- Output goes directly to the Strategy Builder as structured feedback

---

### Agent 2: **Strategy Builder** (`agents/strategy_builder.py`)
**Role:** Strategy generator / idea engine

- Receives the Coach's feedback and generates **batches of strategies** using all indicator combinations
- Covers **4 indicator family groups** across batches:
  - Trend: EMA, SMA, WMA
  - Channel Breakout: Donchian, Keltner
  - Momentum: RSI, ROC, CCI, Stoch, WillR
  - Volatility: Bollinger, Ichimoku, MACD
- Uses **asset class profiles** (from `asset_profile.py`) to tailor strategies -- e.g. for SPX500 (equity ETF) it biases toward trend-following long strategies and avoids mean-reversion oscillators
- Tracks **historical pass/fail rates** per indicator and trigger, and biases generation toward what's been working
- Sends structured strategy JSONs to the Backtester

---

### Agent 3: **Backtester** (`agents/backtester.py`)
**Role:** Rigorous quality gate

- Receives strategy candidates and fetches **historical OHLCV data** (Yahoo Finance, etc.)
- Does **80/20 in-sample/out-of-sample split** for each strategy
- Tests **both long and short directions** and picks the better Sharpe direction
- Runs **hard elimination filters** (fail = disqualified immediately):
  - Max drawdown > 20%
  - Win rate < 35%
  - Fewer than 30 trades
  - Any single trade > 5% loss
  - Losing streak > 8
  - Sharpe < 0.5
  - Only works at 2% max risk level
- **Scores** surviving strategies across 6 weighted dimensions (Risk-Adjusted Return 30%, Drawdown 25%, Consistency 20%, Profit Factor 15%, Trade Frequency 5%, Regime Performance 5%)
- Runs **Monte Carlo simulation** (800 simulations) on each passing strategy
- Applies an **OOS penalty** for low out-of-sample trade counts
- Assigns **confidence rating** (High/Medium/Low) based on OOS vs IS Sharpe ratio and data quality
- Selects **top 3 winners** and passes them to the Trader

---

### Agent 4: **Trader** (`agents/trader.py`)
**Role:** Execution engine

- Receives the winning strategies and **fetches the latest market candles**
- Checks all **exit conditions** on open positions first
- For each strategy, evaluates **entry conditions** using computed indicators
- Enforces **hard safety rules** regardless of strategy:
  - Daily halt if account equity drops 5%
  - 50% position size reduction if weekly drawdown reaches 8%
  - Max 2% account risk per trade
  - Max 6% combined exposure across all open positions
  - Per-strategy halt if that strategy loses > 3% in a day
- Calculates **precise position sizing** from risk parameters
- Places orders via **DryRunBroker** (simulated) or **Alpaca** (real paper trading, currently configured)
- Returns trade log, P&L, and active positions back to the state

---

### Supporting Infrastructure

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `core/network.py` | Message bus routing messages between agents |
| `core/bus.py` | In-memory pub/sub message queue |
| `backtesting/engine.py` | Actual signal/trade simulation logic |
| `backtesting/scorer.py` | Weighted scoring formula |
| `backtesting/monte_carlo.py` | Risk simulation (800 runs) |
| `execution/dry_run.py` | Paper trading broker |
| `execution/alpaca.py` | Real Alpaca paper trading broker |
| `performance/tracker.py` | Weekly/monthly review calculations |
| `performance/feedback.py` | Structured feedback report builder |
| `data/pipeline.py` | Unified candle data fetcher (Yahoo, on-chain, etc.) |
| `agents/asset_profile.py` | Per-asset-class strategy hints |

### Where J_Claw's `backtester.py` Fits In
J_Claw's skill just reads `spx500_cycle_state.json` that agent-network writes and **monitors the active strategy's quality**. It's a watchdog, not a backtester. The real backtesting, selection, and execution all happen inside agent-network.

## Recommended Build Order

### Tier 1 -- Core Game Feel (Do These First)

**1. Haptic Feedback**
Every tap, XP gain, alert, achievement, and rank-up should vibrate with different patterns. This single change makes the entire app feel physical and alive. It's also the fastest to implement (~2-4 hours) with the highest perceived quality jump.

**2. XP Bar Fill Animation + Counter**
When XP is awarded, the bar should visibly fill with easing and the number should count up. This is the heartbeat of the game loop -- if earning XP doesn't *feel* rewarding, nothing else matters. Without this the progression system is invisible.

**3. Rank-Up Full Screen Moment**
The payoff for the entire XP system. Should be a cinematic freeze -- full screen overlay, character animation, particle burst, dramatic text reveal. Currently it just silently updates. This is the moment the whole system is building toward.

---

### Tier 2 -- Reward Moments

**4. Achievement Unlock Overlay**
Same category as rank-up -- a cinematic interrupt when an achievement is earned. Currently just a toast notification. Should feel like a game reward screen.

**5. Swipe Gestures Between Tabs**
Expected on mobile. Swiping left/right between tabs with a slight parallax feel is a huge UX quality signal. Tap-only navigation feels like a web page, not an app.

---

### Tier 3 -- Visual Polish

**6. Custom SVG Tab Icons**
Replacing emoji with custom icons is a visible quality jump. Emoji read as "prototype" -- custom icons read as "product."

**7. Page Transition Animations**
Sliding between tabs instead of fade-up. Makes navigation feel native and spatial -- you build a mental map of where things are.

**8. Particle System**
Confetti/sparks on big XP events, rank-up, achievements. The cherry on top -- comes last because it needs the rank-up moment (Tier 1) to attach to.

---

### Tier 4 -- Nice to Have

**9. Pull-to-Refresh with J_Claw animation**
Custom character animation on pull. Personality touch, not functional.

**10. Chat streaming feel**
Character-by-character text reveal in the chat. Makes responses feel live instead of appearing in a block.

---

**Start with haptics.** It's the fastest win, costs almost nothing, and immediately changes how every existing interaction feels. Then XP animation, then the rank-up moment. Those three alone will take you from 60% to ~85% of the way there.

## My Recommendation: Start with the Data Model, Then Go Full Party Screen

Here's the reasoning:

---

### Why Start with the Data Model (Step 1)

Everything else -- the UI, the activity feed language, the rank-up moment -- all depends on having the character definitions in one place. If you skip this and hardcode character names directly into the HTML, you'll end up with the same name in 6 different places and fixing a typo becomes a project.

One clean config object with all 5 characters, their abilities, lore, class tiers, and stat mappings means every other step just reads from it. It's 1-2 hours of work that saves days of refactoring later.

---

### Why the Party Screen is the Right Second Step (Not the Home Tab)

The J_Claw tab is self-contained. You can rebuild it completely without touching the home tab, trading card, alerts, or any other running system. It's the safest place to prototype the full JRPG feel.

If it looks wrong or the concept needs adjustment, you fix it in one file. Once the party screen is polished and you're happy with how the characters feel, **then** you propagate character names to the home tab and activity feed -- because by then you'll have confidence in the design decisions.

---

### What I'd Skip (For Now)

- **Morale/HP bars** -- interesting but complex. Streak data needs careful mapping and it risks feeling confusing before the core identity (names, classes, lore) is established
- **Activity feed language** -- high impact but lower priority. The party screen will deliver the JRPG feel first; the feed is polish on top
- **Status system** -- RUNNING/IDLE already maps cleanly to ON QUEST/RESTING, this is mostly a label change and can be done in the same pass as the home tab rebrand

---

### The Order I'd Execute

| Step | Time Estimate | Why |
|------|--------------|-----|
| Character data model | ~1-2 hrs | Foundation -- do this first |
| Party screen UI | ~4-6 hrs | Core identity -- biggest visual impact |
| Character sheet (tap to expand) | ~2-3 hrs | Depth -- makes characters feel real |
| Rank-up class advancement cinematic | ~2-3 hrs | The payoff moment |
| Home tab division card rebrand | ~1 hr | Propagate identity outward |
| Activity feed language | ~1 hr | Polish pass, last |

Total: **~12-16 hours** for a complete, polished JRPG party system.

---

**Short answer:** Build the data model first so you only define each character once, then build the party screen so you can see and feel the full concept before touching anything else. Want me to start on the data model now?
